speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want me continue this?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and my father was left in the ghetto. I found out-- and we were sent to Auschwitz. I found out later that he was the end of the month in Bochnia, and then they sent-- [INAUDIBLE] and the rest of them, they sent them to Plaszow concentration camp. And he was in Plaszow till about '45, beginning of '45 he was executed. Have you seen This same commander, Goth, Goth, he shot him. He just-- he used to go around in the ghetto and he shot people at at random.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out that--?

speaker: interviewee
text: From survivors who survived Plaszow. The told me about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out what happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: About '46. I happened to find somebody who was there, and he knew me and he told me about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also mentioned that you had a sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she was picked in 1942. She went to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was even before your mother's death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before my mother, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, as far as you know, did not survive Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody survived. Treblinka was-- nobody came back from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the date of her death?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- it was-- I believe it was in June of 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was she selected?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same thing. They picked names. The Judenrat picked the names and they came and they-- with the police. The Judenrat came to the houses and tell them, you have to report. Of course, people didn't know it. Everything was so-- the Germans were such experts on deceiving people. They told them, get you clothes, winter clothes, because you're going to be resettled in the east, up east. And you're going to work, so-- and it's cold there. So you have to have good clothes, warm clothes. I remember my mother even-- she was constantly expecting mail from my sister. She believed them that she went-- she was always worried why we didn't hear from her anything, and everybody thought-- didn't know it. By the end of 1942, I remember this one boy escaped Treblinka. Yeah, one boy. He looked very-- he looked like a Gentile. He was blonde and for somehow-- anyway, he escaped. This is the only case we know. And he came to the ghetto, to the Judenrat, to the Jewish council, and told the people, don't let yourself deceive. The people-- nobody comes back from Treblinka. And he said people actually are being gassed and cremated and killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, the Judenrat did not believe it. And they even told him to be quiet because he might create panic in people and so they didn't want him to tell anybody. They didn't believe it. We didn't believe it. We always were told-- can you conceive something like that? They're taking women and children in the tens of thousands and gas them and kill them? Nobody believed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believed it when I arrived to Auschwitz, when I saw the crematoriums and I could smell the flesh and the smoke. I realized it that it was fact. It was the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come to hear this boy's story who had left Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he came into our-- to our house, and we had a gathering there, in our house. The Judenrat came and he told us. He had to be very secretly because they didn't want people to know it, and it was in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember this boy's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rothblatt, Rothblatt. Shepsl Rothblatt. R-O-T-H-B-L-A-T-T. I remember it very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were deported to Auschwitz, you were taken from a-- from Bochnia ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. What happened, they took us from the ghetto and not direct to Auschwitz then. They moved us to another camp, which was-- the name was Szebna, S-Z-E-B-N-A. And we were there about a week, and then they took the whole camp, and then they sent us to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you from the Bochnia ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. Just like they grabbed us. You couldn't take nothing, just what you had on.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said the year was 1943?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the month?

speaker: interviewee
text: October.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got to the other camp, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there about a week, and now all the sudden after a week I remember the SS came, a whole regiment, hundreds of them. They enclosed the ghetto-- that camp. And they gave orders, everybody get lined up. We're going to be transferred. They didn't say where we're going. And whole day and then they put us on trains, closed train, cattle trains. And fortunately for us, it wasn't too far from Auschwitz. It took us only one day. But some others weren't so fortunate. I know people came from Greece and Romania, Hungary, which took them several days in a closed-- in a closed cattle train, and many of them just suffocated and died in, in the car. But us, it took us only one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did people talk about on the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're talking about escaping. We tried, but it was impossible. The little window-- I remember it with wire. And we were talking about escaping, but it was impossible. And everybody-- many of them were resigned and they said Kaddish and prayers because you just felt that-- [AUDIO OUT]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the train stopped and the doors opened, what did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was dark. It was late at night. When we arrived all you could see is-- we could see dogs-- SS men with their dogs and some of the inmates who were helping them. And they came in with dogs, and screaming and hollering, everybody raus, raus, you know, typical. We all went out, and I remember the first time that I jumped, jumped out the train. I could smell the flesh. It was unbearable, the human flesh. And you could see from the distance. It wasn't that far, maybe a few hundred feet where you could see the chimneys. They were belching smoke and fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even at night you could see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even at night. It was working day and night, 24 hours a day. And we had to line up in I think in a row, five, and five. You could see from the distance. I could see a little light, and I could see a few SS men. And I could see Mengele. See, he was-- he did the selection. You heard of Joseph Mengele.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember, I was one of the last wagons. And some of the inmates who spoke Yiddish told us, be sure to tell him you're older because he looked at me. He said, how old are you? I told him I was 16. He said, tell him you're older. When you're [? stay ?] before, tell him you are 19 or 20. And give him a profession. Tell him you're a carpenter, you know, a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother was with me and this other fellow, friend of mine who was also with me-- Mendle Braunhut was his name. And we lined up, and just when we got to Mengele. And I stood at attention before him and I said, I tell him I'm 19 years old, and I'm a-- I'm a carpenter. He looked at me just split second and someone, fortunately, put me to the left. My brother, the same, to the left, and my other friend. And I could-- even though it was dark, we could notice-- to the left, I noticed the people there, mostly elderly and children. I said to my brother, we are in the wrong lane. Something, instinct told me. And I could see the other lane, it was already moving. The right one was already moving to the camp, so I said to him, split seconds, we don't have much time. Let's give it a try and jump, run, even though they had SS men with dogs. And I just took off quickly, split second. I even noticed a dog was after me, grabbed me by the-- by the-- by the back, by the leg.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I was too fast, and just a split second-- had it been another five seconds, the other line would have been gone already. And my brother followed me. The third fellow who was my friend, evidently he didn't. He just couldn't make it. And we got into the camp. And the rest of it went to the crematorium. It was-- I don't know what it. It was luck? Or maybe it was fate? Just a matter of seconds, I wouldn't have been here. How were you processed once you got to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got into the camp, first they put-- it was late at night, so they put us in the warehouse. It was cement and bitter cold. It was already very cold. And, by the way, I think I said it wrong. It was in November, not October. It was in a November when I got there, November the 8th. And we stayed there the whole night. And next morning they came, we had to go out again, stay outside for hours. It was already snowing. And finally, they cut our hair. We had-- all our clothes, we had to-- they take over everything. And they cut our hair, and they tattooed us right there. Whole day, we stayed there. And I remember then we had to take showers. Cold water. They didn't have no hot water. They gave us showers. That was-- and then they gave us the clothes, the striped clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody had one jacket, shirt, and most of it-- all of them were in large sizes. Never fit your right. And then they gave us the shoes, those Dutch shoes, remember? And that was the most uncomfortable shoe. The-- I remember I was a size seven. They gave me a size 12. That's all they had, large sizes. And then they assigned us to barracks. What was your number?

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the barrack name or number that you were assigned to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Assigned barrack 11. I was-- it's still there. I was there two years ago. The barrack is still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the warehouse that you slept in the first night, about how many-- was it just men? How many men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just men. They always separated men from the women.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many men, approximately, were in that warehouse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Several hundred of us were in there, several hundred. Might have been maybe 200, 300 of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in barrack 11?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barrack 11.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened next when you got to the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got to the barracks, and we were assigned there. Finally, second day they gave us something to eat, soup. We were all starved. We didn't eat for three days. Finally, they gave us-- and I still remember that soup, even though it was just potato soup. It was-- tasted so good. [LAUGHS] I still remember the taste. And they assigned us to barracks. Those barracks were filthy, dirty. You cannot imagine. We had triple bunks, one and six in a row in the barracks. Actually, which-- those barracks were built by the Polish army before the war for horses. They converted it for human beings. Barracks like that maybe could hold 100 people. They put about 600. We were like sardines, packed like sardines. All they had is straw on the bunks, no blankets. It was warm because we huddled up each to each other, but I could see the lice were just-- literally ate us alive. I was there for six weeks in Birkenau. That was the worst. That was the pits. We didn't have any job. This is-- they called it quarantine, it means transition camp, like basic training. That was the Birkenau. That was Birkenau. We had to get up 5 o'clock in the morning. The kapos came in with sticks and bats, and they used to-- and they used to club us, beat us. And you could see them. They had so much fun. I just-- it had only one door, and here you've got so many hundreds of people and everybody was trying to run, get to be first, get out. And they stood there by the door, about four of them, and with the sticks and bats. It was-- I'm telling you. And you could see it every day. It came to the point I didn't take off my clothes. I just slept in my clothes and didn't take off. So when they came, 5 o'clock, I wanted to be the first so I wouldn't get clobbered. And that was daily routine. That was daily routine. And then had to go out. They had-- in the barracks was no sanitation whatsoever. And here you got 600 to 700 people and 9 o'clock was curfew. You couldn't go in, and you couldn't get out. And here, after all, we were human, and many of us needed to relieve ourselves. So most of them relieved themselves in the barracks. And then when they find out-- the stench was unbearable. And then they came, and if they found out somebody, you know, relieved themselves, they clobbered them to death-- literally killed. They called them schwein, you schweinhunde. They couldn't help themselves. Those people couldn't help themselves. And that was about six weeks. And all I did-- we had to stay outside. They had nothing to do for us. But they just tortured us. They had quarries with stones. I remember big, heavy stones. So we had to pick up the stones, carry them. And the streets were muddy, it wasn't paved. There was about two feet of mud. Put them on another pile, and then pick it up, other pile, bring them back. And it was just, just to torture us. In the beginning, I couldn't comprehend that humans can, human race can stoop so low. You looked at inmates. Everybody-- they were skinny skeletons. And there was no friendship, everybody was dog-eat-dog. It was literally a jungle-- a jungle. It was survival of the fittest. That was Birkenau. It was unbelievable. This-- actually they made-- they degraded us, dehumanized and humiliated us. We became, literally, animals. People looked on the street. Anything, just to-- any scrap you could find on the-- any-- trash cans, anything. All your mind was concentrated on food. You know, they gave us in the morning-- all they gave us is a half a pound of bread, and the bread was mixed with sawdust. And it was-- most of the time, it was hard as a rock because it was mildew because they kept it in the warehouse for months. And all they gave-- and a piece of margarine, which it wasn't real margarine. It was some kind of substitute. And a cup of coffee, chicory coffee. It was cold, no sugar. And that was all they gave you in the morning to eat. And in afternoon they gave you a little soup. It was impossible to survive with this kind of food. The average lifespan was six weeks, from four to six weeks. Most of them never made it. And then the, the suicide rate was unbelievable there in Birkenau. The boys just fell apart. Like I say, the only way you survived, you had to have an extraordinary strong will to live. If you didn't, you just didn't make it. And every morning, it was very simple. All you had to touch them, the wires. You know, the fences were electrified. So every morning you could see dozens of boys electrocute themselves, and that was daily. Fortunately, after six weeks, I knew I couldn't make it longer. I noticed from the distance, I saw a whole line. They picked some boys from next to-- Buna. Buna was about 10 miles from Auschwitz, which was a more-- a little better camp. Over there, you worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they treated you a little better. Here, we didn't work. They just tortured us, so they didn't give us nothing to eat and complete torture. So I volunteered, and I went into the group. And fortunately they sent me to Buna. And, of course, Buna was also part of Auschwitz. But at least the barracks were cleaner, warm, and it was much-- compared to Birkenau, it was like a sanatorium.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you had to work very hard. And, and fortunately from me-- like I say, in Auschwitz, to survive, you had to have a strong will to live and you had to have the breaks. I had a break. That was my first break in Auschwitz. Of course, then Auschwitz-- the first few months was-- even in Buna was very bad. We worked outdoors. And here you only a thin jacket, thin-- no sweaters, no heavy underwear. And we did-- we had to dig ditches. We had to lay, I remember, pipes and like-- and telephone lines. It was bitter cold in Auschwitz. You see, Auschwitz, when you look at the map, is, is in the same latitude as Minnesota. And it usually get bitter cold, often 10 to 20 below zero. And here you have those-- you work outdoors with this little jacket and no-- most of them, just so many of them, literally froze to death. Many of them just contracted pneumonia. And you know, Auschwitz, if you got sick, the only way, the only solution is for you to die because they didn't give you any medication. Also the water-- the water was also very polluted. It wasn't purified. And I remember from the beginning, the inmates told us to not drink the water-- the older ones who were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for a while we didn't drink the water, but after a while you can't help yourself and you start drink the water. And many of us got diarrhea, and diarrhea was a killer. It was a killer. You see, with no medication and you couldn't hold even the little food they gave you. You couldn't hold in it [INAUDIBLE]. And you start to lose weight and became a Muselmann. They call it-- Muselmann was the expression, skeleton. And once you became a skeleton, you can't work. They had no use for you, the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you knew it's just a matter of time, and Mengele used to come in for inspection, selections, right into the barracks every few weeks. You had to get rid of the sick ones, the weak ones who cannot perform anymore to make room for the new ones. We had to undress ourselves naked, stark naked in the barracks. And we had to stay before him. We had to turn around and stoop over. And if he noticed the bones sticking out, that was your death sentence.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took him number down. You know, we all had numbers. And they took the boys and locked them up in the next room. And they never even give them back the clothes. And I remember it was in winter, and it was bitter cold. And you could hear them screaming, crying. We knew-- you can imagine the pain and agony of those boys. We couldn't help them. The next morning the trucks came in, they load them up like trash, like sack potatoes. And they took them right into the gas chambers, gassed them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember every time we knew he is coming, the fear was-- you never knew, you might be next. So we had one solution. I remember we used to drink water. I used to drink buckets of water before he came. Fill myself up so we'll have a big belly. Evidently, it did help, but the next morning we all felt sorry because we all got sick. But we also had a remedy for that. Bread used to get swapped for potatoes. Used to be a lot of black market in Auschwitz. People did all kind of thing. And you took the potatoes, and took it to work. And we charcoal-burned it. And that stopped the diarrhea. You know, we found out from different people. So, evidently, maybe that's the reason I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Mengele used to come into the barracks, was that at Auschwitz-Birkenau, or was Auschwitz-Buna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Buna. That was-- he came Birkenau too, but that was in Buna.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned when you got to Auschwitz, your brother was with you. Was he with you in Auschwitz-Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time, all the time. Buna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he also made it to Buna?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Buna. We were sent to together to Buna. We stuck together and we tried. And maybe this had a lot to do with why we survived because he came into my barracks at night. I came to him. We always consoled each other, and because anytime when I was down, down in the dumps, he tried to help you encourage you. And I encouraged him because we had quite often we planned to commit suicide. [CRYING] And he discouraged me from that and I discouraged him from that too. And we survived till the end. And then add to my misfortune, and I have to tell you that on August the 20th, the American bombed Buna. You see, Buna was a tremendous-- the IG farm industry had a tremendous complex there. That's what we did, the work for the IG farm industry. They made synthetics and all kind of things. That's what we worked in. August the 20th, the American bombed, bombed. It was on a Sunday, I never forget it. And you know, the Germans, they had bunkers right there around there and any time an alarm sounded, they went to the bunkers. We had to stay right in the warehouse. We were working in a building. And I remember that the American bombers came. There must have been about 200 of them. This was the first-- we heard alarms before that, but they never came there. And this time, they came. I remember when, when the alarm sounded, we had to leave the work and we had to go into the buildings. And most of all we were happy because we didn't have to work and everybody laid down. And I remember the first time when I could hear-- it was after an hour, we laid on the cement floor and everybody was joking, laughing. And then all of sudden he could hear heavy flak-- you know, they had flak on the aircraft. Something, instinct, told me that this time it's for real. It's not like last, last few times. And the closer their planes got, they could hear their flak got heavier, heavy. And then you could hear the planes, woo, woo. It was from the distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was-- remember, we were about 300 or 400 inmates. And I was way in the middle. Something told me-- I remember from school-- any time there's an attack, alarm, don't stay in the building. Stay by the door. And I just got up quickly and I ran to the door. As I got to the door, bombed. It was a direct hit on the building, direct hit. And I got hit-- you can see my neck. You see here? I had a splinter in my neck about that big.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, at that time I didn't feel it. It was-- I could hear the noise-- the noise was terrifying. It was a direct hit, and I jumped out. They would stay right by the door. And fortunately, another few guys, we ran out the building. Got away. And the building literally collapsed under me. And we ran out, outside, maybe about 10, 15 feet. And I could see a big crater. It must have been a brand-new crater. A bomb hit it. And I jumped in there and another few guys.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was still hot. We could feel the ground was still hot from the bomb. And we laid there for about 10, 15 minutes. And I could see those planes. They were flying so low. And the bombs were falling from every direction. The earth was trembling. It was terrifying. I could-- literally, I could see the pilots. I could see them from the cockpit, you know? They were flying so low.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from the back you could hear, the screaming and hollering. There must have been-- most of them were buried alive. Many of them killed and maimed. It was terrible. We couldn't help them. After a while-- and I was laying there. I was in shock. I didn't know whether I could hear the noise and everything, but I was in shock. I think that's-- yeah. OK.
